The buyer cited AI maturity, full configuration, and Supplier Portal capabilities as areas of strong interest, signaling dissatisfaction with Ariba's functional gaps. However, no quantified business impact was stated: no cost, headcount, cycle-time, error-rate, or savings figure anchors the business case. Without a quantified value case, Zycus cannot benchmark ROI or build an economic argument to overcome IT's political push for SAP. The next move is to engage the Decision Maker or Economic Buyer (CPO) and quantify the cost of Ariba's 79 pain points in dollars, time, or operational impact.

champion.narrative: No champion is confirmed. Chee Koon Tan (Associate Director, Decision Maker) is the most engaged stakeholder: he led the 25 March discovery call, articulated NUS's pain with Ariba, requested 4 detailed module demos with functional experts, and scheduled the April-June evaluation sessions. However, his advocacy is passive: he has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer (CPO Pang, who has never joined a call), has not pushed back on IT's Ariba bias, has not shared internal evaluation scoring, and has not communicated with Zycus in 40 days (last activity 22 May). His role is functional gatekeeper, not executive sponsor. Without access to the CPO, without evidence of internal advocacy (e.g. coaching Zycus on IT politics, sharing competitor intel, driving the Zycus narrative inside NUS), and with 40 days of silence after the critical evaluation sessions, Chee Koon Tan is a cooperative contact, not a champion. The champion strength is weak. The next move is to re-engage Chee Koon Tan, confirm the evaluation outcome, and request an introduction to the relevant stakeholder to develop true executive sponsorship before the tender opens end-2026.

competition.narrative: NUS is benchmarking Zycus against four competitors in the open tender: SAP Ariba (incumbent), Coupa, GEP, and iValue. SAP Ariba is the primary competitor and the most dangerous: despite 79 documented pain points and a defective NextGen version, the IT department is 'very insistent we give SAP a chance,' giving Ariba political cover. NUS has a 1-year Ariba contract extension, so switching costs and IT integration inertia favor the incumbent. Coupa and GEP are also in the evaluation; no sentiment or positioning intel on them has been captured. iValue is a fourth competitor (source: prior sweep memory). The wedge to win is AI and automation superiority: NUS cited 'AI maturity' and 'full configuration' as top interests, and Ariba is 'too manual' with no working NextGen AI story. Zycus must position Merlin (negotiation agent, intake automation, supplier intelligence) as the AI-native alternative that solves the 79 pain points Ariba cannot fix, and must neutralize IT's political bias by securing CPO-level economic sponsorship. However, no competitive review or battle-card activation has been logged in 40 days, and the April-June evaluation scoring is unknown, leaving Zycus blind to whether Ariba, Coupa, or GEP outscored Zycus on any module.

identify_pain.narrative: NUS's procurement organization (owned by the relevant stakeholder and managed day-to-day by the relevant stakeholder Koon Tan) is experiencing acute operational pain with SAP Ariba. The buyer documented 79 specific pain points with the current system and stated on the 25 March call that Ariba is 'too manual' and 'not able to fix bugs with the new version (NextGen).' The pain is both functional (manual workflows, missing automation) and strategic (SAP's NextGen promise is vaporware, leaving NUS stuck on a defective platform). Key functional gaps cited: lack of AI maturity, incomplete configuration, weak Supplier Portal, and no working solution for unregistered suppliers (utilities, Apple, charities refusing to register on the Zycus Supplier Network). The pain is confirmed and acute, but not quantified: no dollar cost, headcount burden, or cycle-time impact has been articulated. The pain owner is the procurement function led by the relevant stakeholder and operationally managed by the relevant stakeholder Koon Tan.

decision_process.narrative: NUS is running an open tender process launching end-2026 (November/December) per the buyer timeline shared 25 March. The sequence is: market review and vendor demos (March-June 2026, currently in progress), open tender launch (Nov/Dec 2026), RFP response window (January 2027), vendor selection (February-March 2027), negotiation and contracting (April-June 2027), with go-live in Q3/Q4 2027. The Decision Maker (Chee Koon Tan, Associate Director) is leading the functional evaluation and will recommend vendors to the Economic Buyer (CPO Pang). However, the IT department holds veto power: they are 'very insistent we give SAP a chance,' creating internal political tension favoring the Ariba incumbent despite 79 documented pain points. The final approval chain is unclear (whether CPO can overrule IT or must defer), and the Economic Buyer has not engaged to clarify. The call sequence shows only the Decision Maker active; no escalation to CPO or IT stakeholders has occurred. The next move is to map the approval chain and secure CPO sponsorship to counter IT's Ariba bias before the tender opens.

decision_criteria.narrative: NUS is running a structured module-by-module evaluation across four functional areas: sourcing, buying, supplier management, and catalog. The Decision Maker requested 4 detailed demo sessions with functional experts (sourcing lead, buying lead, supplier mgmt lead, catalog lead) to score each module independently, scheduled to begin April 2026 per the 25 March call. The buyer's stated evaluation priorities from the discovery call are AI maturity, full configuration capabilities, and Supplier Portal functionality, with specific edge-case scrutiny on handling unregistered suppliers (utilities, Apple, charities refusing to register). The evaluation is comparative: NUS is benchmarking Zycus against SAP Ariba (incumbent), Coupa, GEP, and iValue. However, no scoring outcome from the April-June sessions has been logged in Salesforce, leaving Zycus blind to whether it passed or failed the functional tests. The next move is to urgently confirm the evaluation scoring with the Decision Maker before the tender opens end-2026.

flags: URGENT: Break the 35-day silence and learn the April-June evaluation outcome. The deal is cooling and Zycus does not know if we scored high enough to be invited to the end-2026 open tender. If the Decision Maker or functional experts scored Zycus materially lower than Coupa/GEP on framework mgmt, supplier registration, or AI maturity, the deal is already lost and no amount of future activity will recover it. Immediate move: Guillaume (regional director, Singapore-based, relationship owner) to call the Decision Maker directly (not email) within 7 days and ask three questions: (1) Did the April-June evaluation sessions occur and how did Zycus score vs competitors? (2) What were the top 2-3 differentiators (positive or negative) the functional experts noted? (3) Will Zycus be invited to the end-2026 tender? If the answer to (3) is no or uncertain, this deal is off-track and requires executive escalation (deal owner's manager to CPO) to salvage. If yes, the deal is on-track but the silence must end.

WIN STRATEGY: Prove Zycus is the ONLY vendor with production-ready agentic AI that solves NUS's operational pain NOW (not roadmap). NUS articulated three high-volume manual pain points (spec generation, recommendation reports, negotiation for tail spend) and explicitly stated 'AI is very relevant for us... the solution for us to handle the extra work.' Zycus has the strongest AI narrative (Merlin 20+ years, agentic orchestration, negotiation agent live with customers), but Guillaume's intro-call positioning was feature-led (agent count, autonomous capabilities, human-in-the-loop) rather than outcome-led (how many FTE hours saved, which customers automated which workflows, proof of ROI). The wedge: bring a live Merlin customer (ST Engineering, FairPrice, MUFG Singapore) to a reference call with NUS functional experts BEFORE the RFP, demonstrate the spec-generation and recommendation-automation use cases in a working production environment (not a demo), and quantify the impact (e.g. 'ST Engineering reduced sourcing-cycle time by 40% and reallocated 2 FTEs from manual report-writing to strategic sourcing'). This proof point must land with the Decision Maker and the 4-5 functional experts who score vendors, AND it must be reinforced in the RFP response to tilt evaluation criteria toward agentic-AI maturity (which Coupa/GEP cannot match at the same production scale).

deltas.subject: MULTI-THREAD + EB ACCESS: Deal is single-threaded through the Decision Maker (Associate Director level) 93 days into Qualified stage, and the Economic Buyer (CPO) has never engaged. In a $250k+ multi-year university SaaS deal replacing an incumbent ERP-class platform, EB passivity at this stage is a red flag: the Decision Maker can score vendors and recommend, but cannot override IT's SAP-preference politics or secure budget sign-off without CPO sponsorship. The 4-5 functional experts (sourcing, buying, supplier mgmt, catalog leads) who participated in the April-June evaluation are also unmapped in Salesforce (only 2 buyer-side contact roles on opp, both C-level). Immediate moves: (1) Request the Decision Maker to introduce Guillaume to the CPO for a 30-min executive briefing on Zycus AI differentiation and APAC customer success, framed as 'ensuring the CPO has visibility into the evaluation outcome before the tender'; (2) Add the 4-5 functional experts as Salesforce contact roles (capture names from April-June session attendees) and identify which expert scored Zycus highest — that person is a champion-development target who can push Zycus internally and help tilt RFP criteria. Without EB access and multi-thread depth, Zycus is vulnerable to a competitor who has built those relationships or to IT convincing the CPO to give SAP one more chance.

RFP influence window closing: buyer plans tender launch end-Q3 2026 (Nov), 4 months from today. Zycus strategy is to 'influence RFP' (27 April Next Step), but 40-day silence and unknown evaluation outcome mean Zycus does not know if they are positioned to shape criteria or already filtered out.

Immediate move: Guillaume (regional director, Singapore-based, relationship owner) to call the Decision Maker directly (not email) within 7 days and ask three questions: (1) Did the April-June evaluation sessions occur and how did Zycus score vs competitors? (2) What were the top 2-3 differentiators (positive or negative) the functional experts noted? (3) Will Zycus be invited to the end-2026 tender? If the answer to (3) is no or uncertain, this deal is off-track and requires executive escalation (deal owner's manager to CPO) to salvage. If yes, the deal is on-track but the silence must end.

WIN STRATEGY: Prove Zycus is the ONLY vendor with production-ready agentic AI that solves NUS's operational pain NOW (not roadmap). NUS articulated three high-volume manual pain points (spec generation, recommendation reports, negotiation for tail spend) and explicitly stated 'AI is very relevant for us... the solution for us to handle the extra work.' Zycus has the strongest AI narrative (Merlin 20+ years, agentic orchestration, negotiation agent live with customers), but Guillaume's intro-call positioning was feature-led (agent count, autonomous capabilities, human-in-the-loop) rather than outcome-led (how many FTE hours saved, which customers automated which workflows, proof of ROI). The wedge: bring a live Merlin customer (ST Engineering, FairPrice, MUFG Singapore) to a reference call with NUS functional experts BEFORE the RFP, demonstrate the spec-generation and recommendation-automation use cases in a working production environment (not a demo), and quantify the impact (e.g. 'ST Engineering reduced sourcing-cycle time by 40% and reallocated 2 FTEs from manual report-writing to strategic sourcing'). This proof point must land with the Decision Maker and the 4-5 functional experts who score vendors, AND it must be reinforced in the RFP response to tilt evaluation criteria toward agentic-AI maturity (which Coupa/GEP cannot match at the same production scale).

MULTI-THREAD + EB ACCESS: Deal is single-threaded through the Decision Maker (Associate Director level) 93 days into Qualified stage, and the Economic Buyer (CPO) has never engaged. In a $250k+ multi-year university SaaS deal replacing an incumbent ERP-class platform, EB passivity at this stage is a red flag: the Decision Maker can score vendors and recommend, but cannot override IT's SAP-preference politics or secure budget sign-off without CPO sponsorship. The 4-5 functional experts (sourcing, buying, supplier mgmt, catalog leads) who participated in the April-June evaluation are also unmapped in Salesforce (only 2 buyer-side contact roles on opp, both C-level). Immediate moves: (1) Request the Decision Maker to introduce Guillaume to the CPO for a 30-min executive briefing on Zycus AI differentiation and APAC customer success, framed as 'ensuring the CPO has visibility into the evaluation outcome before the tender'; (2) Add the 4-5 functional experts as Salesforce contact roles (capture names from April-June session attendees) and identify which expert scored Zycus highest — that person is a champion-development target who can push Zycus internally and help tilt RFP criteria. Without EB access and multi-thread depth, Zycus is vulnerable to a competitor who has built those relationships or to IT convincing the CPO to give SAP one more chance.

value.flag: URGENT: Break the 35-day silence and learn the April-June evaluation outcome. The deal is cooling and Zycus does not know if we scored high enough to be invited to the end-2026 open tender. If the Decision Maker or functional experts scored Zycus materially lower than Coupa/GEP on framework mgmt, supplier registration, or AI maturity, the deal is already lost and no amount of future activity will recover it. Immediate move: Guillaume (regional director, Singapore-based, relationship owner) to call the Decision Maker directly (not email) within 7 days and ask three questions: (1) Did the April-June evaluation sessions occur and how did Zycus score vs competitors? (2) What were the top 2-3 differentiators (positive or negative) the functional experts noted? (3) Will Zycus be invited to the end-2026 tender? If the answer to (3) is no or uncertain, this deal is off-track and requires executive escalation (deal owner's manager to CPO) to salvage. If yes, the deal is on-track but the silence must end.

value.flag: WIN STRATEGY: Prove Zycus is the ONLY vendor with production-ready agentic AI that solves NUS's operational pain NOW (not roadmap). NUS articulated three high-volume manual pain points (spec generation, recommendation reports, negotiation for tail spend) and explicitly stated 'AI is very relevant for us... the solution for us to handle the extra work.' Zycus has the strongest AI narrative (Merlin 20+ years, agentic orchestration, negotiation agent live with customers), but Guillaume's intro-call positioning was feature-led (agent count, autonomous capabilities, human-in-the-loop) rather than outcome-led (how many FTE hours saved, which customers automated which workflows, proof of ROI). The wedge: bring a live Merlin customer (ST Engineering, FairPrice, MUFG Singapore) to a reference call with NUS functional experts BEFORE the RFP, demonstrate the spec-generation and recommendation-automation use cases in a working production environment (not a demo), and quantify the impact (e.g. 'ST Engineering reduced sourcing-cycle time by 40% and reallocated 2 FTEs from manual report-writing to strategic sourcing'). This proof point must land with the Decision Maker and the 4-5 functional experts who score vendors, AND it must be reinforced in the RFP response to tilt evaluation criteria toward agentic-AI maturity (which Coupa/GEP cannot match at the same production scale).

value.flag: MULTI-THREAD + EB ACCESS: Deal is single-threaded through the Decision Maker (Associate Director level) 93 days into Qualified stage, and the Economic Buyer (CPO) has never engaged. In a $250k+ multi-year university SaaS deal replacing an incumbent ERP-class platform, EB passivity at this stage is a red flag: the Decision Maker can score vendors and recommend, but cannot override IT's SAP-preference politics or secure budget sign-off without CPO sponsorship. The 4-5 functional experts (sourcing, buying, supplier mgmt, catalog leads) who participated in the April-June evaluation are also unmapped in Salesforce (only 2 buyer-side contact roles on opp, both C-level). Immediate moves: (1) Request the Decision Maker to introduce Guillaume to the CPO for a 30-min executive briefing on Zycus AI differentiation and APAC customer success, framed as 'ensuring the CPO has visibility into the evaluation outcome before the tender'; (2) Add the 4-5 functional experts as Salesforce contact roles (capture names from April-June session attendees) and identify which expert scored Zycus highest — that person is a champion-development target who can push Zycus internally and help tilt RFP criteria. Without EB access and multi-thread depth, Zycus is vulnerable to a competitor who has built those relationships or to IT convincing the CPO to give SAP one more chance.
